Lesson 8 of 10
Adventure #8—To Sleep, Perchance to Come Up with Great Ideas
Adventure #8—To Sleep, Perchance to Come Up with Great Ideas

Adventure #5—Lights... Camera...
Change the Action
“Hello, I’m Nancy. I guess you’re looking for Tom,” said a woman as I entered the garage. I assumed it was Tom’sAdventure #6—The Write Stuff
wife. “He’s just getting up from a nap and should be coming down in a minute.”
Adventure #7—Oh, What a Tangled
Web We Weave Previous Lesson Next Lesson	Mark as Complete
“Thanks,” I said. “He’s teaching me about the boxes.”	Adventure #8—To Sleep, Perchance
to Come Up with Great Ideas
Adventure #9—The Beginning Nancy gave a knowing smile. “Wonderful things, those boxes.”
Postscript... Four Years Later
“Oh—do you use them?” I asked.
“I certainly do.” She could see my inquiring look, so quickly added: “They’ve been all kinds of help to me around
the house.”
“Oh?” I prodded further.
She continued, “Tom always seemed to have a knack for coming up with all kinds of ideas. I thought he was just
born that way. But one day, the girls and I were in the kitchen making candies. We wanted to make chocolates
with a sweet liquid filling, but we were having trouble getting the filling inside the hollow pieces of chocolate.
“We made all sorts of different holes, but we couldn’t get the filling to go in unless we really heated the filling up.
But then it melted the chocolate.
“Tom came by, looked over the problem and said, ‘Don’t heat the filling. Freeze it.’ So, we froze the filling into
balls, dipped them in chocolate and voilà ! The chocolate hardened, the filling eventually melted back into a
liquid, and we had our filled chocolates.
“Huh,” I responded. “I’d never have thought of that!”
“I know! It never would have occurred to us in a million years to freeze the dang filling. I asked Tom how on earth
he came up with it. When he told me he’d just done the opposite, I asked him to tell me more.”
Her voice lowered to a whisper. “That was when he told me about the boxes, and how he went through a few of
them until he came to Do the Opposite , which, when he applied it, seemed to be the right solution.
“That was when I made him tell me what all the boxes were. And to this day, I still use those boxes to come up
with ideas and solve all sorts of problems.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 6 --

I nodded, happy that we could talk about our shared admiration for her husband’s brilliant idea boxes.
“Like a little while ago, our daughter wanted to start dating. She was definitely ready, but I certainly wasn’t.
Fortunately, I found something in virtually every one of the boxes that helped me deal with the idea using my brain
instead of my heart.”
“How?” I asked with great interest. Perhaps, I thought to myself, these boxes could help improve my relationship
with my own daughters, who were having a tough time of it since my wife had left us.
“Well…for example, Divide It Up made me realize it wasn’t just a question of dating or not dating. We could
divide it up into stages. She could go on dates during the day before going on them at night. She could go on a
date with other couples before she went on a date alone, and so on.
“In Do It Beforehand , I found many things we could do before the dating phase starts that made me more
comfortable with the whole thing. We talked to her about boys—and girls—and dating. We agreed to limits. Both
of us read about teen dating and discussed it afterward. And as a family, we watched a good romantic movie
and talked about it afterward.
“And, of course, Change the Interaction’s Intensity had particular relevance. For example, rather than adding
fuel to the fire by trying to exert parental authority, I realized I could—as Tom might say—create a more ‘inert
interaction’ by making sure she knows why she ought to be careful about situations where youthful passions
might get out of control.”
“That sounds much better than my plan,” I admitted with a sly smile, “which was to lock my daughters in the
basement between their 14th and 21st birthdays.” Nancy and I laughed.
At that moment, Tom came through the door from the house to the garage with slightly puffy eyes. “Who’s locking
who in the basement?” he asked.
“I was just talking to your friend here about how I’ve been using your boxes,” explained his wife. She gave him a
sweet smile as she squeezed past him, going out the same door he had just come through. She called out
behind her, “Nice talking with you. Good luck with your books—and boxes.”
Her sing-song-y voice echoed down the staircase.
Tom looked as if he were still having trouble adjusting to being awake. “Well,” I teased him, “all that inventing
wear you out?”
Tom eyed me suspiciously. “How much sleep do you get?” he asked.
“Not enough,” I replied. “Six and a half hours a night or less. And, unfortunately, no naps.”
“Then I can tell you how to improve your creativity and problem-solving ability quite literally overnight!”
“Oh yeah?”
“Get more sleep! Take naps! If I don’t get enough sleep, a problem I usually solve in 15 minutes can take me an
hour. A report I should be able to write in an hour usually then takes me all day. Plus, I get some of my best ideas
in my sleep.”
I screwed up my face.
“Hey, don’t just take my word for it,” he went on. “You know that riff from the Rolling Stones song ‘Satisfaction’: da
daaah , da da daaah , da DAH DAH da daaah ? It came to Keith Richards in his sleep. He woke up and found he
had dictated it into a little recorder he kept by his bedside. Dimitri Mendeleev came up with the idea for the
periodic table of elements when he saw them all organized in a dream. And August Kekulé realized the
breakthrough ‘ring structure’ of the benzene molecule in his sleep, dreaming about snakes biting their own tails.”
Tom was wide-awake now, and there was no stopping him. “Elias Howe, too, ‘dreamed up’ the idea for the
sewing machine. He dreamt of savages surrounding him with spears, preparing to cook him, which literally
shaped his invention.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 6 --

“But it’s not just coming up with stuff in your sleep. Studies show that you’re a whole lot more creative after you
wake up and as much as three times better able to solve problems—possibly because your mind processes
old junk and clears up processing space while you sleep. Maybe it’s because you just get more stupid the more
tired you get. I guess it doesn’t really matter why , does it?”
“So,” I continued on his wavelength, “‘sleeping on it’ is a good approach to a problem?”
“Absolutely. Just be sure you tell your mind you want it to work on solving whatever specific problem you want
solved. It sounds ridiculous to talk to yourself like that, but it really works. And never try to solve an important
problem if you haven’t had enough sleep. Go take a nap instead.”
“Okay, but please don’t tell this to my employees. They’ll be setting up cots in the storeroom and claim to be hard
at work!” Tom and I shared a chuckle.
“But enough about sleep,” I interrupted myself, rubbing my hands together. “I’m ready for today’s boxes!”
But Tom shook his head and shrugged. “Sorry, I’m all out of boxes. Instead, today I want to show you something
even better—your final, and most important, lesson. But first, think of a problem you’d like to solve.”
“To tell you the truth,” I replied, “my biggest problem right now is that I’m just overwhelmed with all the ideas
you’ve helped me come up with.”
“Good. That’s a great problem to have. Now, I’d like a little fresh air to help me wake up. Why don’t we go for a
walk?”
I nodded in agreement, and we both walked towards the door.
“Keep your problem in your mind,” Tom added, as we stepped outside the garage door and into a glorious
spring day. “Look around. What do you see?”
“I see a beautiful day,” I said, looking around. It was, indeed, a beautiful day, with the sun shining across a bright,
blue sky. “Clear sky, sunny. It’s spring and things are blooming… ”
“And keeping in mind the problem you’d like to solve…” Tom reminded me. “This is where I’d like you to start
making some associations.”
“Hmmm,” I said as I looked around some more. Our neighbor, Pat, waved to us from her garden. We waved
back.
“Things blooming. Plants. Planting. I could ‘plant some seeds’—take a few small steps on implementing a lot of
ideas and see which ones blossom and bear fruit and which ones don’t.”
“Good! Now, what’s going on down there?” Tom asked, pointing towards my feet.
I looked down and noticed some ants at work in the dirt alongside the driveway. Bending down to get closer, I
could see them carrying particles of dirt out of the tunnel and neatly depositing them on the growing mound that
encircled the hole. I became fascinated by the scene.
“Remember the problem…” Tom reminded me again. He was right to, as I had nearly forgotten about it.
“Well, like the ants, I could take all the ideas and apply them one at a time ,” I answered. “Pretty soon, I’d have a
whole pile of good things happening.”
“Yep, that’s another possibility,” Tom agreed. “And looking up?”
I stood up and shielded my eyes from the sun’s bright rays as I looked upwards. “The sun… The sky… A single
cloud floating along all by itself… ”
“Do you know why clouds float?” asked Tom, ever the scientist.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 6 --

“I think it’s because the water molecules are so spread out, and that makes them lighter than the air?”
“Exactly. Any implications for your problem?”
“Floating… Floating to the surface… Maybe I could just kick all these ideas around with my staff and see which
ones ‘float to the surface’—which ones we all get excited about and that the team agrees have the most
potential.”
Silence fell as we continued walking. Tom, clearly giving me time to brainstorm, was carefully watching the
sidewalk. Suddenly, something occurred to me. “Hey! Wait a minute. I thought I was only supposed to think
inside those boxes!”
Tom must have known this was coming. He didn’t flinch before replying: “The boxes are a very useful guide.
They’re a systematic way of looking at things in new ways. But they’re only tools. It’s also important to know that
you can stimulate and inspire your mind on your own—with out the boxes.
“And one of the best inspirations is Mother Nature’s own designs and processes. Got the idea from a fellow
named Alexander Graham Bell.”
At this point, Tom stopped, and reached into his pocket, pulling out a folded-up piece of paper. “I carry his words
around with me to guide me whenever I feel a bit lost. May I?”
I nodded, eager to hear the quote.
Tom started to read:
“‘Leave the beaten track occasionally and dive into the woods. Every time you do so you will be certain to find
something that you have never seen before. Follow it up, explore all around it, and before you know it, you will
have something worth thinking about to occupy your mind. All really big discoveries are the results of thought.’
“Of course,” Tom continued, “it doesn’t have to be nature. You can be inspired by the streets of a city, the scene
outside your window or the construction of a piece of furniture in your office. All that matters is that it gets you
thinking in a new direction.”
“Well,” I replied, shoving my hands in my pockets sheepishly. “You’ve certainly got me thinking in many new
directions. In fact, I don’t think I’ll ever be able to think or look at anything in quite the same way again.
“Is there anything I can do for you in return? Any books you’d like? Free coffee and pastry for life?”
Tom chuckled and batted the air. “No no, don’t be silly. But do I appreciate the offer,” he replied with a slight
smile. “After all—it’s the thought that counts.”
Summary & Exercises
In a Nutshell
This Adventure is about feeding your brain with three essential foods: sleep, water and impressions.
Rest Up!
The need for sleep to support your brain (and brainstorming) should be obvious to anyone who has ever gotten
too little of it and can’t think straight the next day.
But even if you’re feeling almost at your best, here’s the problem. Creativity and ideas depend on connections .
And feeling even just slightly less than 100% can easily mean you’re not making the quality of connections that
will give you the ideas you need to get the job done.
It’s like buying 90% of a one-way ticket from New York to London. It may cost only 10% less, but it doesn’t do you
any good at all. In the same way, if there’s a great idea waiting for you and, due to lack of sleep, you get 90% of
the way to making the connection that reveals it, that won’t do you any good at all. (Close, they say, only counts
with hand grenades and horseshoes.)
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 6 --

The other important thing about sleep is that all sorts of activity goes on inside you while you do it. Your brain is
processing information and forming new connections. Your subconscious mind controls 83% of your
brainpower! Most of your brain is subconscious—and never stops working. That’s why so many people wake up
with great ideas in the morning—or even in the middle of the night.
This leads me to what may well be the most valuable piece of advice I can give you. I know you may have heard
this before, but this time try it, OK?
Never go anywhere without something—be it pen and some paper or the notes app or voice recorder
on your phone—to make a record of your ideas. Not to bed. Not for a drive in the car. Not for a walk or
jog. Nowhere!
You see, because your subconscious brain never stops working, you must be able to record what it comes up
with at a moment’s notice. For some people, the problem is not a lack of ideas but an inability to capture the
ideas they get. Ideas are extremely short-lived and hard to hang onto—there one minute and gone the next—
unless you grab onto them and preserve them on paper or digitally.
So take the time to get familiar with the memo or recording app on your phone and use it to capture ideas––no
matter what you’re doing at the time. When it’s convenient, just play them back and transcribe them.
Drink Up!
In addition to getting enough sleep, you also want to make sure you get enough water . Studies show that your
brain is 30% less efficient if you’re just 2% dehydrated. If you’re 5% dehydrated, efficiency is cut in half (by
50%).
I don’t know about you, but I’m nowhere near smart enough to get by on half my brainpower. So drink up! (Keep
a pen, notepad and a glass of water by your side at all times, and who knows what you can accomplish!)
Look Up!
The final food the brain needs is impressions . Impressions, especially visual ones, are the fodder the brain turns
into new ideas. In a sense, you create your own idea-generating boxes when you let the world around you inspire
and trigger your creativity.
Because just as the 15 boxes in Idea Power suggest different ways for you to think about any problem,
opportunity or situation, what you observe in the room you’re in, the article you’re reading or video you’re
watching, or when you go for a walk outside will suggest new connections, perspectives, ideas and actions for
you to take.
Perhaps you’ll be as lucky as Archimedes, the ancient Greek mathematician who was trying to solve the
problem of how to determine the volume of a solid object (in this case, a solid gold royal crown).
How in the world do you measure the volume of something as intricate as a crown?
It was a tough problem. So he headed off to the bath to relax a bit and take his mind off the problem.
But as he climbed into the full tub, he noticed that the water was starting to overflow. “Eureka!” he cried, as he
ran naked through the streets of the Greek city of Syracuse announcing his discovery.
His discovery is now taught in every high-school physics class: a solid object appears to displace its volume in
water. So by submerging the crown in a full tub of water and measuring the amount of water it displaced as it
overflowed, it would be possible to determine the exact volume of the object.
And poor Johannes Gutenberg, back in the 1400s…
He figured out the moveable type aspect of printing, but all that rubbing on the back of the pages against the
type box to transfer the ink was taking forever .
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 6 --

So, he headed off to a wine festival to get those creative juices (pun intended) flowing. And there, he saw a giant
wine press that used a flat board to press the grapes when a wheel is turned. “Ach du lieber!” he cried. “There’s
my printing press .”
Stuck? Take action! Do something. Go somewhere. And speaking of action, here are your action steps for
Adventure #8…
MODULES
Your Action Steps
1. Select a problem you need to solve. Before you go to sleep tonight, tell your brain to work on it and give you
an answer by morning. If you’re skeptical, that’s okay. But try it. Many people find that this really works . And if it
works for you, wouldn’t it be wonderful to be able to depend on a skill like that? With practice, you’ll be able to.
2. Take the problem you’ve been working on as we’ve made our way through the 15 boxes together and look
around the room. Right now. What do you see? And what might you apply to the question at hand about each
one of the things that you notice? (For example, you see your phone. That might take you to thinking about
communications. Talking. People connected by a network. Something that constantly interrupts. How might each
association apply to your problem?)
Get enough sleep. Sleep fuels creativity. Tell your mind to work on a specific problem while you
sleep. Never try to come up with ideas (or make important decisions) if you’re tired.
Find inspiration all around you for solutions and new ideas.
All of these resources are also available in your Idea Power Mindbook if you prefer to write
them out by hand.
